sentences.text: Yes.
I actually jumped a little bit ahead of myself.
I think around 1942-- and I am not exactly sure about the dates, but approximately-- there was-- apparently the Germans decided that it was time to liquidate the death box.
And so they went about it very systematically.
Up until then, there was attrition.
People just died by attrition, and it was quite rapid.
But after-- at, at that point, we had regular raids where people were just scooped up from the streets and just disappeared.
Certainly we did not trust it, but we hoped against hope.
Oh, they probably take them.
They need people to-- for labor.
And in a way, I think they tried to spread the rumor, oh, that we are taking you just for labor, to labor camps.
But raids-- the way the raids occurred was that they would come, like, I guess, with a battalion of Germans, I think assisted by, by Lithuanians and Ukrainian followers.
But mostly, mostly, I remember German.
There was no way for us to really distinguish them except that they did wear different uniforms.
They did not wear the, the the-- I guess auxiliary-- the soldiers that were Russian-- I mean that were Ukrainian or Lithuanian.
I don't remember seeing any Poles.
They were dressed differently.
And I am not even absolutely clear that I have seen them, their presence, in, in the ghetto, because I-- their presence was very, very clear in the concentration camps.
So it's possible that in my memory, that there is-- that there is some, you know, just merging.
And it may not be absolutely accurate.
But the way they did it with, with, with precise suddenness.
They would just appear, where we did not have a chance to hide.
And first, they would just pick people up from the streets.
And I remember my father came once home.
He was just, just, saying, I don't understand.
Don't they have mothers?
Don't they have children?
How can they-- how can they impose such heinous, such, such terrible torture on children?

And you know, even today when I watch television-- sometimes-- oh, when I watch my grandchildren, when I watch my grandchildren, I sometime have a shutter go through me, and I just cannot understand how they could have done-- how they could have done this to such beautiful promise.
Well, they would come in, in battalions, sort of a double trot, their, their boots against, against, the, the cement or cobblestones.
And you could here the, the, the clanking of, of, of the bayonets and guns and, and, and the triggers.
And shouting, [GERMAN] Out Jews, out.
And, and they did it with such-- with such storm.
And if you escaped it, if they they were satisfied with, with, with their-- with what they hunted up, they would go away.
And then you never knew when-- where it would happen.
At that point, the people started to disappear.
At that point, people started to think of, OK, the most, the most thing that was in the fore-- in your mind was, where do you hide?
So people hid under beds, between, between the blankets, between even mattresses.
And of course, you had to do it so smoothly that they wouldn't-- that you wouldn't be found.
In drawers, in cupboards, in, in, in the most unusual places.
And of course, not-- after a while, they became common knowledge.
So it also became a random thing.
If the Germans poked their bayonet in your bed, if you were found-- and if you were hiding, there you were found.
We-- we-- it was a four-story building.
So everyone in the row, in this one row, one on top of another, we put a, a wardrobe, Anne Frank style.
We put a wardrobe in front of the door.
And all of us in this building, we only stayed in one place.
And that was in my friend, Janka's room.
It was her room.
But all the, all the rooms were disguised by the closets.
And there we sat.
And we, we, we, all we could hear from, from behind the doors is when you click hear the screamings, and the slapping, and the kicking, and the shooting, and the screams.

And, and then the most, most petrifying thing was when they walked through each apartment, and you could hear their boots, or them leaning against the closet, the wardrobe, or moving-- looking inside the wardrobe.
That, that was just-- you know, that was just so, so petrifying because you were just a hair away from, from, from your hunters, from your assailants.
Then, then eventually you would hear the, the, the wailing and the noise.
It recede.
And of course, you're always think-- oh, they are trapping us.
Is it safe to come out?
And then, then, then the real wailing came out because people were afraid to cry.
And when you heard these, these, these, these shrieking cries, these, these most heart-rendering screams, these outcries, then we knew that it was safe to step out.
And then you slowly-- usually we, we would move the, the wardrobe slowly.
Be careful, be careful.
And then we would come out.
And we'd stare into the windows for any sign of light.
And usually, emptiness stared right back at us.
And when we looked out the window in our courtyard, there was one of my neighbors who was pregnant, or the bayonet right through her stomach.
Two children particularly come to my mind because they were my friends.
One boy-- Janusz was his name-- he came out and he just screamed so.
He just sat there and wailed, and wailed, and wailed.
He and his parents were hidden in different beds between-- what you'd call these down comforters-- between the comforters.
His parents were pulled out and he was left.
He was left alone.
He marched on.
We couldn't hold him back.
[CRYING] That was the last of him.
Then there was another incident.
I had a friend, Lola, who lived at, at the building-- we-- that was at right angles to us, on the same floor.
And we were very good friends.
They had a little dog.
They hid.
The dog kept on following the Germans and barking.
Now the German felt ah, maybe the dog is trying to lead him to something.
He did.
The Germans were going away.
Excuse me.
The Germans were leaving, but they returned.
And the whole family was taken.
And then the dog wailed again.
But he wailed because they were gone.
And that was everyday life.
Yes?
You told me before, during the pre-interview, about some of the other ways the Germans found the hiding places, other than, you know, the dog or just randomly stabbing a mattress.
Can you tell me, perhaps, you know, what, what other tools they might have used or whatever assistance they might have gotten in finding the hiding places?
They sometime-- are, are you referring to a friend of my father's who was used to, to-- who they tried to use?
Is that-- or was there anything else that I mentioned?
It's hard for me to keep it all in mind.
Oh, that was much, much later.
That was-- no.
At this point, that was when we were in bunkers, when, when the bunkers were built.
And that, that was considerably later.
And I will get to it.
Don't let me forget.
Then there was-- and I will tell about this friend of my father's later because it did occur later.
And then one day, there was a big round up.
[BLOWING NOSE] The Germans made a special effort.
Excuse me.
[LAUGHS] The Germans made a special effort to, to spread the rumor that everyone who's going to surrender is going to be sent to labor camps and that they'll save their lives.
And everyone who will not will be shot.
Somehow, it, it-- they, they-- see, all the other raids were unannounced.
This was-- this was the only one that I recall that was announced.
And it was a big, big, big raid.
Now, they did tell us where to report.
They also said that you can take some belongings with you.
And since we are going to catch you anyway and send you away, at least this way, you have the advantage that you can take some of your belongings and you can go with your families.
And so we put layer, upon layer, upon layer of clothes.
And we had our little bundles.
And we-- and told us where to report.
And it was a huge, huge field.
It was a big, big notorious raid.
You know, everyone who's experienced-- it was really-- it stood out simply because of the grand scale of it.
And so we came-- you had, you know, these people dragging through, through, with-- through Warsaw.
And everybody is, is descending to assemble in this big field.
And there are the Germans standing in their leather and their whips.
And they are, you know, they, they are absolute masters of your life.
That was the big selection.
What they really did, they told people to line up.
And if they tapped you this way, you go this way.
You, you, you-- then you join the march.
If he taps you that way, you, you stay.
You stay.
You remain.
Fortunately, all four of us-- my mother, my sister, and my father, and I were tapped not to join the march.
But my friend Janka, she was very sick at that time.
She had the yellow jaundice.
She was so yellow, so waxen.
And the Germans pointed for her to go to join the march.
She clutched her pillow, and she yammered, Mama, Mama.
[CRYING] Her mother didn't join her.
[CRYING] But when she was gone and the Germans withdrew, her mother went crazy.
[CRYING] She just screamed and surrendered.
[CRYING] Periodically people said, enough, enough.
And they-- and they-- and they-- they felt that maybe this way, they saved some dignity, that they were so afraid that they are going to become so, so, so, so ravaged.
I remember Janka had a nanny.
And that nanny just dressed up.
She said, I don't want to hide anymore.
She said, I want to look my best.
And she dressed up and took her little suitcase as though she was going away somewhere.
She says, whatever will be will be.
At least I still look human.
And just marched off.
And that was the end.
Yes?
Did you know about the, the exterminations, the gassings, and the shootings?
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
OK.
Well, you see, people were disappearing.
But some people managed to escape.
So people came back.
They came back.
We knew about Treblinka, and that was not far from Warsaw.
I don't know how close it was.
But it was quite close.
Excuse me.
[BLOWS NOSE]
So we were aware of it.
My parents tried to give us to a-- hide us in a-- oh, what do you call it where-- like a church, a convent.
[LAUGHS] They tried to give us to a convent.
And apparently there was some interest because the church felt that it would be a way of saving souls.
And very young children were, were very desirable for that.
So my parents asked us.
And we said, no.
We, we always-- we were a very close family.
And my sister and I pleaded no.
No, we absolutely did not want to be separated.
And so my parents accepted it.
I forgot where I was.
I had asked you if you knew about the exterminations?
Right.
So, so news about-- people came back and they did tell us.
We, we were aware.
To what degree-- we, we still-- you know, that maybe-- still-- there was still an inkling of hope.
And also what there was, as long as you had one another, you lived very often for the other one.
It certainly was true with us.
Our survival was completely dependent on one another.
And I, I, I will mention later some incidents about that.
Yes.
The Einsatzgruppem we heard, too.
And there was one particular incident that we had very direct contact with.
Later on when, when we started to build bunkers-- and that was after the raid.
Maybe I should come back to that, to that-- or I-- I think-- I'll come back to this one example because other thoughts keep on crowding.
And-- After that, after the big raid, the ghetto became smaller and smaller.
And it was shrunken to very-- just a few streets.
There was no wall around that immediate neighborhood where the Jews lived.
There was sort of like a no man's land between that little section of the ghetto and the non-Jewish side.
And you only could live if you had a job, if you had permission to live.
That-- there were shops where we repaired-- German-- mended German uniforms.
And somehow a friend of my sister's father ran a shop that was mending the uniforms.
And he gave us the jobs.
And so, we-- and we were mending uniforms.
And that gave us the legitimacy.
I was, at that time, not quite 13 years old.
I was 12.
Children below 13 were contraband.
They could not exist at all.
They, they could not-- they had to be surrendered.
I was very frightened then.
[LAUGHTER] And my parents-- I had-- I had pony tails and-- pig tails.
And my parents cut my hair and made me look-- fortunately, I was small, but fortunately I was physically developed enough if they dressed me up more grown up, they-- I could pass for 13.
And I remember I was asked once, my father I said, Father, what will you do if they come and take me away?
And he said, what do you mean?
They'll never take you away from me.
If the Germans come and try to take you away, I'll burn their eyes out with acid.
I can tell you how reassuring it was.
At least in his love, I was safe, if, if nothing else, as isolated as I was.
And it gave me tremendous comfort, for whatever it was worth in reality.
At that time, while we were-- we had this legitimate existence, where the very few Jews and the very few Jewish streets were left, people started to build bunkers.
And if you-- the houses that we-- the buildings that we lived in were really pretty empty.
It was a very ghostly-- even though there were only a few streets-- it was very ghostly.
It was, you know, It was only like shadows of life that was all gone.
And we knew quite well where they went.
And we had a bunker too.
Now, there were-- in, in our bunker, that's when we met Mr.
Miodowski, Moisze Miodowski.
I mention his name because nobody heard of him, but should have.
He had a voice that was so divine.
He, he was in operas.
Actually he was a [HEBREW], because he was Jewish.
But he also-- I don't know if he ever performed in operas.
I'm not sure.
My father, he became-- he, he sort of-- Our family took him in.
Now, how did we get to know him?
He drifted in-- I don't know how-- back into the Warsaw Ghetto.
He was with a group of Jews from his little town who were taken to a field.
And they were told to dig their own graves.
And, they were-- and the people were shot, lined up and shot.
I guess to add dignity to the moment, he did what he knew how to do best and he started to sing.
And he sang the [HEBREW]-- not the-- when Jewish people die, [HEBREW].
Isn't it [HEBREW] that you sing to commemorate the death of a person?
You mean Kaddish?
Kaddish.
OK, thank you.
He was-- he was-- he started to sing Kaddish.
He,he sang so beautifully that he moved, he moved a German.
And the German hid him under, under the corpses.
And that's how he-- and, and I, and then somehow worked his ways at night during hidings.
He might have known of the sewers, and I will tell you about the network of sewers in [?
Attica. ?] We communicated, moved from place to place, and possibly this way, but that's how he came to us.
And sometimes in all this darkness, my parents-- I-- we would gather in a small room, and he would sing.
And his voice, it was just-- he gilded our, just moments of existence.
That's, that's Mr.
Miodowski.
Yes?
Were you aware, at this time, of any partisan movements within the ghetto?
Yes.
Now, you know, I think that we all tend to attach a precise date for all events.
But they don't really, I think, happen suddenly.
And I became aware of the, of the resistance movement in-- after-- shortly after the liquidation of the-- of the Warsaw ghetto.
They-- you heard of-- I guess the reason why they became known to me was maybe because I heard it at home.
Because, we did not know, but my father was active as a partisan.
He was not fighting, but he was, as far as my sister and I can now construct-- reconstruct some of the events that we reflect on-- that he was involved very, very early.
And we, we knew that one of the things that they were concentrating on first was to prepare themselves.
They had no, no weapons.
They, they-- so we know that they started to sneak out of the Warsaw Ghetto to obtain-- to establish contact with, with Polish partisans and collaborate with them, or ask them for support.
And to obtain-- and also maybe sometime-- they had to gather whatever little money there was from the Jewish community.
So in some cases, they most probably tried to purchase.
OK.
